critic_raw:
This CNN vs. traditional feature extraction design for dermatoscopic classification has one critical data leakage flaw and several significant methodological problems.

The design explicitly states that normalization statistics are computed from the entire dataset before splitting, then applied to training, validation, and test. This is canonical preprocessing leakage. The mean and standard deviation of the test images have been used to normalize those same test images. In a 10,000-image dataset split 60/20/20, this is a detectable but potentially small effect for pixel statistics — but the design explicitly justifies this by saying it 'provides more reliable normalization.' This argument is wrong: it trades a tiny instability (using only 6,000 images for normalization) for a principled violation of the holdout guarantee. For the traditional feature branch, this is even more problematic, because continuous features like Haralick statistics and Gabor responses can have distributions that differ meaningfully between low-agreement and high-agreement cases. Leaking the test distribution into the scaler parameters is not conservative.

The baseline choice is strategically weak. Logistic regression is the weakest possible discriminative baseline for a multi-class image classification problem with ~80-120 features. A more demanding comparison would be a random forest or SVM with an RBF kernel, both of which are standard in traditional medical image classification and would substantially reduce the CNN's apparent advantage. Choosing logistic regression as the sole traditional baseline is justified in the design as isolating 'feature representation' — but this reasoning only holds if the feature engineering pipeline is optimal. If Haralick/LBP/Gabor features are suboptimally extracted (e.g., wrong scale parameters), then the CNN wins on both feature quality and architecture. These are confounded.

The data filtering on inter-rater agreement >90% introduces a survivorship bias. High inter-rater agreement cases are the easiest cases — consensus is easy precisely when visual features are clear. A model trained and evaluated exclusively on easy cases will show inflated performance relative to deployment on unfiltered clinical images. The design's claim to evaluate 'robustness' to device variation while simultaneously filtering out ambiguous cases is contradictory.

The ResNet-50 fine-tuning procedure is incompletely specified: freezing 'early layers and training only the final residual block and classification head for 50 epochs' is an unusual protocol that could perform very differently from standard transfer learning (full fine-tuning from some layer, or feature extraction only). The sensitivity of results to this choice is not evaluated. A poorly chosen freezing boundary could substantially degrade CNN performance, making the comparison uninterpretable.

Class weighting is mentioned as a control for class imbalance during training, but the evaluation metric (macro F1) does not require class weighting for validity — and if class weights are applied differently to the CNN (end-to-end) vs. logistic regression (explicit weight parameter), the comparison is no longer on equal footing.
all_issues_raised: Normalization statistics are computed on the full dataset before splitting and applied to the test set, introducing preprocessing leakage that violates the holdout guarantee — justified in the design by a stability argument that is methodologically incorrect.

Logistic regression is a strategically weak baseline for a ~80-120 feature multi-class problem; the CNN advantage conflates feature quality and model capacity since the feature engineering pipeline is not separately optimized.

Filtering exclusively to >90% inter-rater agreement cases trains and evaluates on the easiest subset of the clinical distribution, producing inflated performance estimates relative to real deployment.

The ResNet-50 fine-tuning boundary ('final residual block and classification head') is an unusual protocol whose sensitivity to the freezing choice is not evaluated, making CNN performance contingent on an unvalidated architectural decision.
Class weighting applied during training but not reflected in the primary evaluation metric (macro F1) can diverge between models if the weighting schemes interact differently with each architecture.
